summary: Stancil argues that Snowflake’s new application framework signals a shift from “data apps” as dashboards on top of the warehouse to apps that run inside the warehouse itself. If Snowflake executes this vision, it becomes more like an iPhone platform than an app store, enabling deeper functionality, easier distribution, and a new wave of data products. He concludes that this could reignite the data tooling ecosystem rather than mark its decline.
money_quotes: Data apps are actually much more than blinged-out dashboards that sit on top of a warehouse.
They’re also programs that run in a data warehouse.
Snowflake isn’t just an app store for buying widgets; it’s the entire iPhone.
Installing a data app would be like downloading a mobile app: Put in a credit card, check a few permission boxes, and you’re ready to go.
Running apps inside of a database could redefine what a database is as much as iPhones redefined what cell phones were.
key_insight: Snowflake’s app framework points toward a future where apps run inside the data warehouse, potentially turning Snowflake into a platform layer that reshapes data infrastructure and product ecosystems.
